methodology: Cross-referenced CalorieKing, FatSecret, USDA FoodData Central, MyFitnessPal community averages, and EatThisMuch. Local Chinese restaurants don't publish nutrition facts, so all values are ESTIMATES based on typical recipes and ingredient-level USDA data. Portions reflect typical takeout containers (~2 cups / 400-500g for entrees). Calorie ranges account for variation in oil usage across restaurants.

items.notes: Plain steamed long-grain white rice. Baseline carb source. Most takeout portions are 1.5-2 cups. USDA #20044.
items.lean_version: Swap to brown rice (+2g fiber, +1g protein). Or request half portion rice, double vegetables.

items.notes: Long-grain brown rice, cooked. Similar calories to white but significantly more fiber and micronutrients. Not always available at Chinese takeout. USDA #20040.
items.lean_version: Already the lean option. Portion control is the lever — request 1 cup instead of the standard 1.5-2 cups.

items.notes: Rice stir-fried with egg, soy sauce, oil, peas, carrots, diced chicken. Oil content varies widely. Typical takeout pint container.
items.lean_version: Ask for light oil or steamed rice + chicken on side. Saves 150-200 cal. Or request brown rice fried rice if available.

items.notes: Similar to chicken fried rice but pork adds slightly more fat. Diced BBQ pork or char siu common.
items.lean_version: Swap pork for chicken or shrimp. Request light oil. Saves ~50 cal from protein swap + 100-150 from oil reduction.

items.notes: Shrimp is leaner than chicken/pork so slightly fewer calories. Still high due to oil in frying process.
items.lean_version: Request extra shrimp, light oil. Best fried rice option protein-to-calorie wise.

items.notes: Combination of chicken, pork, shrimp, and sometimes beef. Highest protein but also highest fat of fried rice options.
items.lean_version: Skip the combo, pick single lean protein (shrimp or chicken). Request light oil.

items.notes: Soft egg noodles stir-fried with chicken, cabbage, bean sprouts, soy sauce. Oil-heavy sauce base. One of the most popular takeout orders.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra vegetables, light sauce, light oil. Or swap to steamed chicken + veggies with sauce on side. Saves 200+ cal.

items.notes: Beef adds more fat than chicken. Flank steak or sirloin typical. Noodles absorb oil heavily.
items.lean_version: Swap to chicken lo mein (saves ~40 cal) or request steamed beef with vegetables.

items.notes: Leanest lo mein option. Shrimp keeps protein decent while lowering fat vs chicken/beef.
items.lean_version: Request extra shrimp, extra veggies, light oil. Best lo mein option.

items.notes: Fewer calories but very low protein. Mostly noodles + cabbage, carrots, bean sprouts. Not ideal for body composition despite being 'lighter'.
items.lean_version: Add a protein source (steamed chicken or shrimp on side). The noodles alone won't keep you full.

items.notes: Crispy fried noodles (vs soft lo mein). More textural crunch, similar calories. Bean sprouts, celery, onion common. Some restaurants use soft noodles — ask.
items.lean_version: Request soft noodles instead of crispy (less oil in frying). Ask for extra vegetables, light sauce.

items.notes: Beef version is fattier. Similar noodle base as chicken chow mein.
items.lean_version: Swap to chicken chow mein. Request soft noodles, extra vegetables.

items.notes: Wide flat rice noodles stir-fried with beef, bean sprouts, scallions. Very oil-heavy — the wide noodles absorb a LOT of oil. 'Dry-fried' style uses less sauce but same oil.
items.lean_version: One of the hardest to make lean. Request light oil (won't reduce much). Better swap: get steamed rice noodle rolls (cheung fun) if available.

items.notes: Thin rice vermicelli with curry powder, shrimp, pork, egg, peppers. Curry gives it yellow color. Rice noodles are lighter than egg noodles. One of the better noodle options.
items.lean_version: Request extra shrimp, skip the pork. Light oil. Rice vermicelli is naturally lighter than egg noodles.

items.notes: Thai-Chinese overlap item. Rice noodles with tamarind sauce, egg, bean sprouts, peanuts. High sugar from tamarind sauce. Peanuts add fat.
items.lean_version: Skip peanuts (saves ~80 cal). Request sauce on side. Or get it with shrimp instead of chicken for slightly fewer calories.

items.notes: Deep-fried battered chicken in sweet-spicy sauce. One of the HIGHEST calorie Chinese dishes. Breading + frying + sugar sauce is a triple threat. Extremely popular.
items.lean_version: Swap to Chicken & Broccoli (saves 350+ cal). If you must have it, ask for sauce on side and eat only the chicken pieces, scraping excess sauce. Or request 'dry-fried' without batter.

items.notes: Deep-fried battered chicken in sweet orange sauce. Even more sugar than General Tso's. The orange glaze is essentially candy coating on fried chicken.
items.lean_version: Swap to Chicken & Broccoli or Chicken with Mixed Vegetables. If craving citrus, try Lemon Chicken (slightly less sauce). Sauce on side saves 100+ cal.

items.notes: Deep-fried battered chicken with sweet sesame glaze. Nearly identical to General Tso's nutritionally. Sesame seeds add minor calories.
items.lean_version: Same as General Tso's — swap to stir-fried (not fried) chicken dish. Or ask for steamed chicken with sesame sauce on side.

items.notes: Diced chicken stir-fried (NOT battered/deep-fried) with peanuts, dried chilis, bell peppers, zucchini. Much better than General Tso's because no breading. Peanuts add healthy fats but also calories.
items.lean_version: Request fewer peanuts or skip peanuts (saves ~80 cal). Extra vegetables. One of the better 'indulgent' choices since it's stir-fried, not deep-fried.

items.notes: Stir-fried chicken with cashews, celery, water chestnuts, light sauce. Similar to Kung Pao but less spicy, less sugar. Cashews are calorie-dense but not as bad as breaded dishes.
items.lean_version: Request fewer cashews (saves ~60 cal). Extra vegetables. Good option — stir-fried, decent protein.

items.notes: Deep-fried battered chicken with sweet & sour sauce (sugar, vinegar, ketchup). Pineapple chunks, bell peppers. Very high sugar. The sauce alone can be 150+ cal.
items.lean_version: Request sauce on side. Better yet, swap to Chicken & Broccoli. If you need the sweet-sour flavor, dip lightly rather than drowning.

items.notes: THE lean Chinese takeout order. Stir-fried chicken breast with broccoli in light garlic/oyster sauce. No breading, no heavy sugar sauce. Best protein-to-calorie ratio on the menu.
items.lean_version: Request steamed with sauce on side (saves 100+ cal from oil). Ask for extra broccoli. This IS the lean version of Chinese takeout.

items.notes: Stir-fried chicken with mixed vegetables (broccoli, snow peas, carrots, mushrooms, baby corn, water chestnuts). Light sauce. Similar to Chicken & Broccoli but more veggie variety.
items.lean_version: Request steamed with sauce on side. Extra vegetables. Tied with Chicken & Broccoli as best lean option.

items.notes: Battered, fried chicken with sweet lemon sauce. Less sugar than orange chicken but still a fried/sauced dish. Some places do a lighter version with sliced chicken in lemon sauce (not battered).
items.lean_version: Ask if they can do it with sliced chicken (not battered). Sauce on side. Saves 200+ cal if not deep-fried.

items.notes: Grilled or pan-fried chicken thigh/breast with teriyaki glaze. NOT battered. Teriyaki sauce has significant sugar (soy + mirin + sugar). Better than deep-fried options.
items.lean_version: Request sauce on side, use sparingly. Ask for chicken breast instead of thigh. Grilled is better than pan-fried. One of the better options.

items.notes: Sliced flank steak stir-fried with scallions in sweet soy/hoisin sauce. Sometimes lightly battered. High sugar sauce. Green onions are the main vegetable.
items.lean_version: Request not battered, light sauce, extra scallions/vegetables. Sauce on side saves significant sugar calories.

items.notes: Sliced beef with broccoli in oyster/garlic sauce. The beef version of the classic lean order. Higher fat than chicken & broccoli but still a strong choice. Sauce is lighter than sweet options.
items.lean_version: Request steamed with sauce on side. Extra broccoli. Or swap to Chicken & Broccoli to save ~60 cal from leaner protein.

items.notes: Sliced beef with bell peppers, onions in savory brown sauce. Good vegetable content. Not sweet-sauced. One of the better beef options.
items.lean_version: Request light oil, extra peppers/onions. Sauce on side. Good as-is — one of the leaner beef choices.

items.notes: Sliced beef stir-fried with mixed vegetables (broccoli, snow peas, carrots, mushrooms, water chestnuts). Light sauce. Solid lean choice for beef lovers.
items.lean_version: Request steamed with sauce on side. Extra vegetables. Very similar to Beef & Broccoli nutritionally.

items.notes: Deep-fried battered pork in sweet & sour sauce. Pineapple, bell peppers. Very high sugar and fat. Pork is fattier than chicken version. One of the highest calorie options.
items.lean_version: Swap to Sweet & Sour Chicken (saves ~30 cal from leaner protein). Better yet, skip sweet & sour entirely and get stir-fried pork with vegetables.

items.notes: Stir-fried shredded pork with cabbage, mushrooms, egg, wood ear, scallions. Served with thin Mandarin pancakes (add ~60 cal each) and hoisin sauce. Without pancakes, actually a decent option.
items.lean_version: Skip the pancakes (saves 120-180 cal for 2-3 pancakes). Use lettuce wraps instead. Light on hoisin sauce. The filling itself is relatively lean.

items.notes: Cantonese roasted BBQ pork with sweet honey/hoisin glaze. Red-colored exterior. Good protein but high sugar from marinade. Typically served sliced over rice.
items.lean_version: Request lean cuts (less fatty pieces). Good protein source. Pair with steamed vegetables instead of rice to keep total calories lower. Sugar is in the glaze — can't avoid it.

items.notes: Shrimp in egg-based white sauce with ground pork, garlic, ginger, peas. No actual lobster — 'lobster sauce' refers to the Cantonese egg sauce. Rich and creamy but not sweet.
items.lean_version: Request less oil in sauce. The egg sauce is the calorie driver. Ask for extra shrimp, fewer peas. Good protein content already.

items.notes: Shrimp stir-fried with vegetables in spicy garlic sauce (chili-garlic, soy, vinegar). Mixed vegetables (broccoli, carrots, water chestnuts, zucchini). Good option — spicy, savory, not sweet.
items.lean_version: Request light oil, extra vegetables. Sauce on side. One of the best shrimp options — savory sauce with no heavy sugar.

items.notes: Shrimp stir-fried with peanuts, dried chilis, bell peppers, zucchini. Same preparation as Kung Pao Chicken but with shrimp. Peanuts add calories.
items.lean_version: Request fewer peanuts. Extra vegetables. Shrimp is already lean — this is a solid option.

items.notes: Shrimp stir-fried with broccoli in light garlic/oyster sauce. THE leanest Chinese takeout entree. Best protein-to-calorie ratio. Pair with rice for a complete meal.
items.lean_version: Request steamed with sauce on side. This is already the leanest option on any Chinese menu. Add steamed rice for carbs.

items.notes: Deep-fried wheat wrapper filled with cabbage, pork, carrots. Typical order is 2 rolls = 440 cal. Wrapper absorbs significant oil during frying.
items.lean_version: Swap to spring rolls (vegetable, not fried). Or get steamed dumplings instead. Each egg roll skipped saves 220 cal.

items.notes: Smaller than egg rolls, thinner wrapper, vegetable filling. Still fried but less oil absorption due to thinner wrapper. About half the calories of an egg roll.
items.lean_version: Fresh (not fried) spring rolls with rice paper wrapper are ~60 cal each. Ask if they have fresh rolls.

items.dish_name: Crab Rangoon / Cream Cheese Wonton (1 piece)
items.notes: Deep-fried wonton wrapper filled with cream cheese and imitation crab. Typical order is 6-8 pieces = 560-740 cal. Very calorie-dense for the size. Mostly fat and refined carbs.
items.lean_version: Limit to 2-3 pieces max. No good lean swap exists. Skip entirely and get soup or edamame instead.

items.notes: Chicken broth with egg ribbons, cornstarch thickener, scallions. Very low calorie. Good starter to fill up before the entree. High sodium (~800mg per cup). Takeout container is usually ~2 cups.
items.lean_version: Already very lean. Start with a cup before your entree to reduce total meal calories. Skip the fried noodle garnish if offered.

items.notes: Chicken broth with tofu, bamboo shoots, wood ear mushrooms, egg, vinegar, white pepper. More substance than egg drop soup. Tofu adds protein. Spicy and sour flavor. ~900mg sodium per cup.
items.lean_version: Already lean. Great starter. More filling than egg drop soup due to tofu and vegetables.

items.notes: Pork/shrimp wontons in clear chicken broth with bok choy, scallions. Wontons are ~25 cal each. Takeout container usually has 8-10 wontons (~240 cal total container). Better protein than egg drop.
items.lean_version: Already relatively lean. Ask for extra broth, extra bok choy. Good option — protein from wontons, low overall calories.

items.notes: Steamed soybeans in pod. Excellent protein and fiber for an appetizer. Usually lightly salted. One of the best starters — high protein, high fiber keeps you full. USDA #11212.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest appetizer option. Skip the salt if watching sodium. Great pre-meal choice to reduce overeating on the entree.

items.notes: Steamed pork/chicken/shrimp dumplings with thin wheat wrapper. Typical order is 6-8 pieces = 300-400 cal. Much better than fried version. Dipping sauce adds ~10 cal per tbsp (soy-vinegar).
items.lean_version: Already the lean version. Steamed >> fried. Choose shrimp or chicken filling over pork for slightly fewer calories.

items.notes: Same filling as steamed but pan-fried until crispy bottom. Typical order is 6-8 pieces = 480-640 cal. The frying adds 30 cal per piece. Crispy-bottomed 'guotie' style.
items.lean_version: Swap to steamed dumplings (saves 30 cal per piece = 180-240 cal per order of 6-8). Same filling, same dipping sauce, major calorie savings.

items.notes: Nearly zero calories. Main concern is sodium (~900mg per tbsp). Low-sodium soy sauce has ~600mg. Use freely for flavor without calorie guilt. USDA #16123.
items.lean_version: Use low-sodium version. Calorie-wise it's a free condiment. Great for adding flavor to steamed dishes.

items.notes: Sweet plum/apricot-based condiment. Pure sugar essentially. Comes in those little packets — each packet is ~1 tbsp. People use 3-4 packets = 120-160 cal of pure sugar.
items.lean_version: Use soy sauce or hot mustard instead (nearly zero calories). If you must, limit to 1 packet. Each packet saved = 40 cal of sugar eliminated.

items.notes: Thai-style sweet chili sauce. Very similar to duck sauce nutritionally — mostly sugar with chili flakes. Popular dipping sauce for spring rolls and dumplings.
items.lean_version: Swap to sriracha (5 cal/tbsp) or soy-vinegar dip (10 cal/tbsp). Same flavor category, fraction of the sugar.

items.notes: Thick, sweet, fermented soybean-based sauce. Used in Mu Shu pork, Peking duck, stir-fries. Sweet and salty. High sugar for a condiment.
items.lean_version: Use sparingly — a little goes a long way. Mix with soy sauce to thin it out and reduce sugar per dip.

items.notes: Savory, slightly sweet sauce made from oyster extracts. Very low calorie for a sauce. Used in most stir-fried vegetable and meat dishes. The 'secret sauce' in Chicken & Broccoli.
items.lean_version: Already very low calorie. One of the best sauces nutritionally. Request oyster sauce-based dishes over sweet sauces.

items.notes: Chili-garlic hot sauce. Very low calorie condiment. Some sugar but negligible in normal serving sizes. Great for adding heat and flavor with almost no calorie cost.
items.lean_version: Already lean. Use as a replacement for duck sauce or sweet chili sauce — adds heat with 70% fewer calories.

tier_1_best.description: Under 400 cal per serving. Best protein-to-calorie ratio. Order these with steamed rice.
dishes: Chicken with Mixed Vegetables (370 cal, 30g protein)
Shrimp with Garlic Sauce (380 cal, 26g protein)

tier_2_solid.description: 400-550 cal per serving. Good options with some calorie awareness.
dishes: Beef with Mixed Vegetables (430 cal, 28g protein)
Mu Shu Pork without pancakes (430 cal, 26g protein)

tier_3_indulgent.description: 550-700 cal per serving. Treat meals. Watch the sauce and breading.

tier_4_calorie_bombs.description: 700+ cal per serving. Deep-fried + sugar sauce combo. Save for rare occasions.

biggest_wins.notes: Same protein source, skip the breading and sugar sauce. The #1 swap.
Still flavorful and satisfying. Stir-fried vs deep-fried. Peanuts add crunch.

biggest_wins.swap: Sweet & Sour Pork → Mu Shu Pork (no pancakes)
biggest_wins.notes: Skip the breading and sugar sauce. Lettuce wraps instead of pancakes.
Steamed rice (206 cal) vs fried rice (530 cal). Add protein separately.
440 cal → 300 cal. More pieces, more protein, fewer empty calories.
Per 6 pieces: 480 → 300 cal. Same filling, skip the oil.

biggest_wins.swap: Duck Sauce (3 packets) → Soy Sauce + Sriracha

general_tips: Start with soup (65-120 cal) to fill up before the entree arrives
Request 'sauce on the side' for any dish — dip, don't drown
Ask for 'light oil' or 'less oil' — restaurants use 2-4 tbsp oil per dish (240-480 cal from oil alone)
Brown rice has same calories as white but 6x the fiber — request it
Skip the fried noodle garnish on soups (adds 50-80 cal of nothing)
Typical takeout entree + rice = 600-1000 cal. Plan accordingly.
The 'combo plate' with fried rice + egg roll + entree can easily hit 1400+ cal
Chopsticks naturally slow eating pace and portion control vs fork
Leftovers strategy: order a full entree, eat half with rice now, save half for tomorrow
